summary: DeVault argues that Linux is not accessible to average users today and examines what would need to change if the community wants mainstream adoption. He uses a racecar analogy to distinguish between expert-oriented software (which is valid) and software marketed to non-experts, arguing Linux excels for programmers but has failed ordinary users. He identifies three areas needing improvement: robustness, intuitiveness, and community culture. He criticizes the toxic elitism in Linux communities, the proliferation of bad advice blogs, and upstream projects that guide users toward expert-level installation methods. Rather than copying Windows, he proposes building something intuitive on its own terms, and suggests a commercial general-purpose Linux distro as the business model that could align incentives with end-user needs.
key_points: Linux is the best OS for professional programmers but has failed to serve non-expert users, and the community must decide if mainstream accessibility is even a goal worth pursuing
Desktop Linux projects prioritize shiny features over reliability and polish — a small, intuitive, reliable program beats a large, unintuitive, unreliable one
The Linux community's toxic elitism and flood of bad-advice blogs actively harm new users and embarrass the ecosystem
Linux should develop its own intuitive paradigms rather than copying Windows, letting its unique strengths shine through accessible design
A commercial general-purpose Linux distro with paid support could create the right incentives to prioritize average end-users
money_quotes: Linux is not accessible to the average user today, and I didn't need to watch these videos to understand that.

It is silly to suggest that Formula 1 vehicle designs ought to accommodate non-expert drivers, or that professional racecar drivers should be driving mini-vans on the circuit. However, it is equally silly to design a professional racing vehicle and market it to soccer moms.

Linux is the operating system developed by programmers, for programmers, to suit our needs, and we have succeeded tremendously in this respect. However, we have failed to build an operating system for people who are not like us.
A small, intuitive, reliable program is better than a large, unintuitive, unreliable program. Put down the paint brush and pick up the polishing stone.
Linux is a box of loosely-related tools held together with staples and glue.
Given a hundred ways of doing things on Linux, there will be 99 assholes ready to tell you that your way sucks.
It's very easy to find bad advice for Linux, and very hard to find good advice for Linux.
You can sell free software — someone ought to do so!
key_insight: Linux's mainstream failure isn't technical but structural: the incentives of volunteer developers building for themselves will never produce a system for non-experts, and only a commercial model with dedicated support can bridge that gap.
